Mirroring my reply from direct cast PR to 202605 (https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-sairedis/pull/1955#issuecomment-4842198142):

@StormLiangMS , regarding your concerns:

> I have one concern before this merges: the PR description scopes the fix to Broadcom management-port `N/A` counters, but the implementation changes generic `FlexCounter` behavior. In the single-counter path, any `collectData()` failure now publishes zero-valued counters to COUNTERS_DB, which can mask real SAI read/clear failures on normal objects and makes unsupported/error/true-zero indistinguishable.

I agree that there is a risk where failures are masked away - this is precisely why this was a temporary workaround limited to specific branches.  This is a compromise that has been acknowledged when this temporary workaround is done for the 202412 and 202505 branch. Counter values also cannot go negative as unsigned ints are used for its storage, and anything not a number will cause issues in testing (i.e. `N/A`). So the only way to make the value appear as a number wihle making sense is to set it to 0.


> Could you either scope the zero-fill to the known Broadcom management-port unsupported-counter case, or confirm why it is safe for all single-counter `FlexCounter` failures on 202605?

The existing FlexCounter implementation is unable to distinguish between ports - there is no notion of multiple ports where all ports are treated as the same. This is precisely why the full fix is needed fast.
Different topo/hwskus will have different number of interfaces, and interfaces are referenced by `VID`, which is generated during runtime. Ordering of interfaces in the vector of `VID`s is also not garunteed to match the port/lane numbering of the switch. This means there is  no good way to check for a port's type without making SAI calls and comparing an interface's counter support with another, which you may notice is similar to the full fix...

Any sort of check on interfaces will involve so many parts that it will either be extremely inperformant or become the full fix itself.

> One minor thing as well: the new `SWSS_LOG_INFO` messages use `0x%x` for `rid`/`vid`; these are `sai_object_id_t`-sized values, so please use `PRIx64` or `sai_serialize_object_id()` like nearby logs to avoid truncated IDs.

Will do.

Changed the title.

> **Review:** #1948 (re-review at `6fcb8c8`)
> 
> Thanks for the revision — verified all the changes against the full source, not just the diff. Everything I flagged is genuinely resolved, and the fixes are correct (not just relocated). Dismissing my earlier CHANGES_REQUESTED and approving.
> 
> **#1 (blocking correctness) — fixed correctly.** `processPendingDampingSync` now _only_ enqueues; it no longer touches `pending_state_sync` or `advertised_status`. The in-memory commit moved into `flushPendingDampingNotifications` (lines 1616-1617) and — crucially — sits **inside the success path, after `m_notifications->send()` returns**. So a batch dropped by the overflow guard or failed in the `try/catch` leaves `pending_state_sync = true`, and the next pass re-enqueues it. The permanent advertised/physical mismatch (and the strictly-worse overflow-drop case) is now self-healing. Exactly the retry semantics I was asking for.
> 
> **#2 / the gating issue — fixed.** `processPendingDampingSync()` + `flushPendingDampingNotifications()` are now called unconditionally on both the TIMEOUT and unknown-selectable paths; the `hasAnyValidDampingConfig()` gate is gone, so a stranded queue always drains on the next loop iteration. This also removes the redundant double O(ports) scan.
> 
> **#4 (DRY) — fixed** via the new `serializePortVids()` helper, used by both paths. The Copilot swap-vs-copy nit is also addressed (`emplace()` + `swap` at enqueue).
> 
> **New surface checked:** moving the commit into the send path means `flush` now touches both `m_pendingNotificationsMutex` and `m_linkEventDampingMutex`. Confirmed there's **no lock-ordering inversion** — both `processPendingDampingSync` and `flush` acquire one mutex, release it, then acquire the other; neither ever holds both simultaneously. The `try/catch` around the send (one bad batch can't strand the rest) is a nice touch.
> 
> The core decoupling remains correct: `m_notifications->send` is main-thread-only, the timer thread only sets flags under the damping mutex. This is a sound fix for the `select failed: 2` race. LGTM.

Thanks [lolyu](https://github.com/lolyu)

## Multi-model review — [vpp][fdb] Add fdb support

Thanks for this — the core VPP FDB push-notification design is solid: the producer (VPP events thread) only touches the MAC-event queue mutex, the consumer drains under `m_apimutex`, the single FIFO preserves event ordering, and the FFI boundary is POD-only with `memcpy`/`memcmp` for the MAC key. A few things to address before merge.

### Should-fix

**1. `l2_macs_event` batch is truncated at 128, dropping FDB deltas** (`vslib/vpp/vppxlate/SaiVppXlate.c`, `vl_api_l2_macs_event_t_handler`)
`l2_macs_event` carries `mac[n_macs]` with a `u32` count. When `n_macs > VPP_MAC_EVENT_BATCH_MAX (128)`, the handler logs an error and drops entries `[128..n)`. FDB events are state deltas, not telemetry: a dropped age leaves a stale MAC, a dropped learn means orchagent never learns it. `max_macs_in_event=10` makes >128 unlikely in practice, but please consume the full batch (e.g. process in chunks and invoke the callback repeatedly) rather than truncating.

### Questions

**2. Teardown ordering of `deinitFdbEventHandling()`**
The MAC-event callback is deregistered at the end of `~SwitchVpp`, after the aging thread is already joined. This is safe as long as `staticMacEventCb` is only ever dispatched on `m_vpp_thread` (which `~SwitchVpp` joins first). Can you confirm VPP's socket client doesn't deliver unsolicited `l2_macs_event` on a separate internal rx thread? If it can, there's a teardown window where the callback could run during/after destruction. Hoisting the deregistration to the start of `Sai::uninitialize` would remove the ambiguity.

**3. Warm-boot reconciliation of `m_vpp_fdb_entries`**
The snapshot map is in-memory only and isn't persisted to the warm-boot file. After a warm reboot it starts empty while VPP's L2FIB still holds entries, so an age event for a pre-reboot MAC won't be in the snapshot and no aged notification is generated (stale MAC). How is the snapshot reconciled with VPP's L2FIB across warm boot?

### Scope — unrelated change bundled here

**4. `syncd/scripts/syncd_init_common.sh`** contains two changes unrelated to VPP FDB and not mentioned in the description:
- The CONFIG_DB key read by the init script is renamed `orch_southbound_zmq_enabled` → `swss_zmq` (that key was added recently in #1835). If the producer side isn't renamed in lockstep this would silently disable southbound ZMQ on every syncd platform. Please confirm the coordinated change and call it out.
- nvidia-bluefield hugepages bumped `11700` → `16000`.

### Nits
- Process-global FFI state `g_mac_event_cb` / `g_mac_event_ctx` / `g_mac_event_batch` vs per-switch `initFdbEventHandling`: the last registration wins and one switch's teardown nulls the callback for all. Latent today (single-switch), but worth an assert/comment documenting the single-switch assumption.
- ADD path records `m_vpp_fdb_entries[key]` even when `generateFdbLearnedOrMoveEvent` early-returns on a port-resolve failure; a later DELETE then calls `generateFdbAgedEvent`, which won't find it in `m_fdb_info_set` and logs an ERROR on a benign race. Consider only recording the snapshot on a confirmed-generated event, or downgrading that log.
- `Meta::objectHasAttribute()` (new in `meta/Meta.{cpp,h}`) has no production caller — it's only used by `TestMetaDash.cpp`.
- The `Sai.fdbAgingWakeEvent` unit test is effectively a start/stop "doesn't deadlock" smoke test (100ms sleep, no MAC events injected); the comment over-states that it exercises the wake path.
- The action codes `0`/`1`/`2` (ADD/DELETE/MOVE) are duplicated across the C struct comment, the trampoline, and the C++ switch — an enum would prevent drift.

## Follow-up review — [vpp][fdb] Add fdb support (re-reviewed at `94d2e66`)

Thanks for the rework — re-reviewed the updated branch. The items from the previous round are addressed:

- ✅ **Batch handling** — the `l2_macs_event` handler now consumes the full batch in chunks and invokes the callback per chunk, so events beyond 128 are no longer dropped.
- ✅ **Scope** — the unrelated `syncd_init_common.sh` and `meta/Meta` changes are gone; the PR is now a focused saivpp/vslib diff.
- ✅ **ADD snapshot** — `m_vpp_fdb_entries[key]` is now recorded only on a confirmed-generated event.
- ✅ **Teardown ordering** — `deinitFdbEventHandling()` now runs first in `~SwitchVpp`, before the events thread is joined.
- ✅ **Single-switch global cb** — documented + `assert`.

A few live items remain:

### Should-fix
**`m_swif_to_port_id` cache is never invalidated.** `resolvePortIdFromSwIfIndex` memoizes `sw_if_index → SAI port OID` permanently — there's no `erase`/`clear` on the map. VPP recycles `sw_if_index` values after an interface is deleted, so the next interface reusing that index resolves to the *previous* port's OID. That mis-attributes FDB learn/move/age notifications, and `vpp_fdb_entries_invalidate_by_port` matches against the same stale cache. The companion `m_swif_to_bdid` is already untracked on port-leave via `swif_bdid_untrack` — suggest invalidating `m_swif_to_port_id[sw_if_index]` in the same place.

### Questions
1. **Teardown thread model.** Deregistering before the join is safe as long as `l2_macs_event` is only ever dispatched on the WR() polling loop (per the code comment). Can you confirm VPP's socket client never delivers it on a separate internal rx thread? If it can, there's still a window where `staticMacEventCb` could touch a half-destroyed switch.
2. **Warm boot.** `m_vpp_fdb_entries` / `m_fdb_info_set` are in-memory only. After a warm reboot they start empty while VPP's L2FIB still holds entries, so an age for a pre-reboot MAC hits the "unknown MAC, skipping" path → no aged notification → stale FDB_TABLE in SONiC. How is the snapshot reconciled with VPP's L2FIB across warm boot, or is FDB warm-boot explicitly out of scope for saivpp?

### Nits
- The single-switch `assert(g_mac_event_cb == NULL ...)` is compiled out under `NDEBUG`; a second registration would silently clobber the global in a release build. Consider a runtime log + early-return alongside the assert.
- `SwitchVpp.h` comment references `onVppMacEvent()`; the actual producer is `staticMacEventCb`.
- The unit test exercises the aging thread's start/stop lifecycle; the queue-drain path in `processFdbEntriesForAging` isn't directly covered.

## CI Failure Analysis — Build [1129081](https://dev.azure.com/mssonic/be1b070f-be15-4154-aade-b1d3bfb17054/_build/results?buildId=1129081)

**Not PR-induced. Caused by [sonic-net/sonic-platform-vpp#225](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-platform-vpp/pull/225) (Trixie migration).**

### Root Cause

[sonic-platform-vpp#225](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-platform-vpp/pull/225) ("sonic-platform-vpp trixie migration", merged 2026-06-02 20:36 UTC) updated the VPP build from Bookworm to Trixie. The sairedis CI pipeline uses `runVersion: 'latestFromBranch'` to pull VPP artifacts, so it now downloads Trixie-compiled packages. The CI agent still runs Bookworm (glibc 2.36), causing:

```
libvppinfra depends on libc6 (>= 2.38); however:
  Version of libc6:amd64 on system is 2.36-9+deb12u14.
```

### Timeline

| Time (UTC) | Event |
|------------|-------|
| 20:24 | PR #1921 build [1129024](https://dev.azure.com/mssonic/be1b070f-be15-4154-aade-b1d3bfb17054/_build/results?buildId=1129024) downloads VPP `26.02-rc0~143` (Bookworm) → ✅ **passes** |
| 20:36 | [sonic-platform-vpp#225](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-platform-vpp/pull/225) merges → VPP artifacts updated to `26.10-rc0~31` (Trixie) |
| 22:05 | PR #1922 build [1129081](https://dev.azure.com/mssonic/be1b070f-be15-4154-aade-b1d3bfb17054/_build/results?buildId=1129081) downloads VPP `26.10-rc0~31` (Trixie) → ❌ **fails on glibc mismatch** |

### Impact

All sairedis PRs using the current pipeline will fail. PR #1923 (unrelated change) has the same failure.

This PR only modifies `syncd/scripts/syncd_init_common.sh` (a shell script) — completely unrelated to VPP or glibc. Will re-trigger CI once the pipeline is fixed.

cc @dypet — the corresponding buildimage change is https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/pull/27013, but the sairedis CI pipeline still runs on a Bookworm agent.

## CI Failure Analysis — VS Test Stage

The P4RT/VS test failures on this PR are **not PR-induced**. They are caused by a systemic issue affecting all sonic-sairedis PR builds since ~Jun 22.

### Root Cause

The `_INCLUDE_DOCKER` restructuring of `docker-sonic-vs` (merged to sonic-buildimage master Jun 17) renamed the supervisor program from `[program:start.sh]` to `[program:start]`. The DVS `check_services_ready()` in `sonic-swss/tests/conftest.py:588` still looks up `"start.sh"`, which no longer exists — causing the readiness check to time out after 60 seconds and all DVS-based tests to fail with `AttributeError: NoneType has no attribute get_logs`.

### Fix

[sonic-swss PR #4696](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-swss/pull/4696) addresses this by accepting either `"start"` or `"start.sh"`. Once that merges, the sairedis CI pipeline will pick up the updated sonic-swss and DVS tests should pass again.

### This PR

Unit tests and lint checks pass. The only failure is the VS test stage, which is blocked on the fix above. A CI retry after #4696 merges should go green.

Hi @sivat6 — I've been working on the same feature (#1798 is already merged and I closed #1935 in favor of this). I looked at the CI failures and have a few things that might help.

The Azure pipeline uses `-Wshadow -Werror`. CodeQL alert #482 flagged `portVidStr` in `applyAiedAlgorithm` as shadowing another variable — that's likely your build blocker. Renaming one of them (e.g. `strPortVid`) should fix it.

`isRedisPortAttribute` in `RedisRemoteSaiInterface.h` still has `sai_object_id_t` as the first parameter type — the name typo was fixed but the type should be `sai_object_type_t` (callers pass an enum). This could also hit under `-Wconversion`.

For CodeQL alert #481, the issue is that `applyLinkEventDamping` checks `algorithm == DISABLED` before the switch and then has the same case inside the switch. Removing the case from the switch (keeping only the early return) should clear it.

Two non-blocking observations: the `s->select(&sel, 1000)` change makes the main syncd loop poll every second even when no ports are damped — a timerfd that only fires when needed might be lighter. And `writeDampingCountersToStateDb` on every 1s timer tick for each damped port could get noisy at scale; writing only on state transitions might be enough.

My CLI and YANG PRs (sonic-net/sonic-utilities#4367, sonic-net/sonic-buildimage#26231) are ready to complement this once it merges.

Summary:
- Adds a documentation file (.github/agents/pr-vs-triage.agent.md) defining an AI agent spec for triaging VS test failures on sonic-sairedis PRs. No code/CI behavior changes; purely docs.

Reviewed by: AI agent on behalf of Ying.

Non-blocking nits:
- Consider a brief validation note (e.g., tested against a recent failed build) since the doc includes specific ADO commands/URLs.
- Some hard-coded ADO GUIDs/URLs may be brittle; add guidance to re-derive or mention fallbacks (e.g., artifact zip).
- Minor inconsistencies/redundancy: repeated read-only constraints, placement of “download artifact” step, inconsistent “vstest/VS test” terminology, minor grammar.
- The referenced “Known Flake Catalog” isn’t included; add a starter catalog or remove the reference.
- Optional: add a lightweight version/date header or clarify .github/agents/.agent.md consumption conventions.

Good spec overall — the PR-diff scoping table (§4a) and cross-build correlation workflow are the strongest parts and directly solve the "innocent PRs getting blamed" problem.

A few things worth addressing:

**Substantive:**

1. **"Known Flake Catalog" is referenced but never defined.** Section 5 uses it as a classification signal but there’s no catalog anywhere in the file or repo. Either add a starter list inline (even just a handful of well-known recurring flaky tests like PortChannel LACP, p4rt redis-socket), or remove the reference and rely solely on the cross-build correlation in §4b which is already well-defined.

2. **Step 4 (download docker-sonic-vs artifact) is misplaced.** Downloading the full docker image belongs in the "Local repro" section, not in the log-fetching triage workflow — it could cause an agent to pull ~1 GB unnecessarily when it only needs task logs.

3. **No validation note.** "How did you verify/test it?" says N/A. A quick note like "validated against build #XXXXXX for PR #YYYY" would confirm the `curl`/timeline/`log@1` paths actually work as described.

**Minor:**
- Terminology: "vstest", "VS test", "VS-test" used interchangeably — pick one
- Read-only rule is repeated 4 times; once in Hard Rules is enough
- ADO project GUID is hardcoded in Lesson 4 — worth adding a note that it can be derived directly from the check `link` URL
- `log@1` size warning (Lesson 10) should also appear inline at the §3e download step since an agent might happily pull ~1 GB mid-triage

Hi @lunyue-ms, thanks for sharing! I wanted to provide some updates — I did some research on the log to look into why the `[port]` case (Ethernet sub-interface) is also failing, not just `[port_in_lag]`.

Seems like the test expects ICMP replies from the DUT's sub-interfaces (e.g., `Ethernet64.20`, `PortChannel1.20`), but VPP never responds.

Looking at the code, the root cause is in `SwitchVppRoute.cpp` — when a connected subnet route is installed with `next_hop_type = ROUTER_INTERFACE` (which is the normal case for directly-connected IPs on sub-ports), the IP programming call is commented out:

https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-sairedis/blob/88bc51ae95df66977601957515e5527119ffd4c5/vslib/vpp/SwitchVppRoute.cpp#L148-L151

This was commented out in the upstream `sonic-platform-vpp` repo during the Debian Bullseye migration (https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-platform-vpp/pull/22, see [here](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-platform-vpp/pull/22/changes#diff-cbfb8019729fef861ce1c0660547a346f35b41a768d91c62c0c7ac6cf4a71f6bL171) for the exact line of change).

I believe the the original `vpp_add_del_intf_ip_addr` function couldn't handle non-PORT interface types (LAG, loopback) and hard-rejects anything that isn't `SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_PORT`. The assumption was that VPP's LCP plugin would handle IP sync from Linux → VPP for all interfaces, which works for regular ports but doesn't appear to work for sub-interfaces.

There are several directions out of this:

1. Wire the existing `vpp_add_del_intf_ip_addr_norif` into the `ROUTER_INTERFACE` case (this function already resolves interface names from prefixes and handles both PORT and LAG sub-ports).
2. Fix the original `vpp_add_del_intf_ip_addr` to handle LAG/loopback types, then uncomment.
3. Fix LCP sub-interface pairing so IP sync works implicitly (longer-term, likely a WG discussion topic).

A targeted hot-fix would be option (1) scoped to only `SUB_PORT` RIF types, so it doesn't risk regressing the existing LCP-based flow for regular ports. LCP improvements could be addressed separately as a longer-term item.

I think this is out of scope for this PR (which addresses LAG hwif name resolution) and would also live in a different repository. Happy to look into a follow-up hot-fix PR for this if it would be helpful.

@lunyue-ms I have pushed some additional commits that should help with the `routing_between_sub_ports` and `balancing_sub_ports` tests.

For the `tunneling_between_sub_ports` tests, the tunneling feature is simply not implemented. Since its a relatively large feature (likely several hundred lines C++), I believe it deserves its own dedicated feature PR.

Please let me know if the new changes helped, thanks!

**Note:** The previous subport removal implementation called `delete_sub_interface()` followed by `refresh_interfaces_list()`. The new approach strips IPs and admin-downs instead, preserving the VPP sub-interface object.

The motivation was to avoid `refresh_interfaces_list()` during removal, which is suspected as a contributor to the test failure in `routing_between_sub_ports_unaffected_by_sub_ports_removal`, since it destroys and rebuilds the entire local cache. Admin-down + preserve is a lighter-weight approach that sidesteps the issue. The trade-off is that removed sub-interfaces accumulate as admin-down stubs in VPP, bounded by unique VLAN IDs per parent (max 4094). 

If this approach still doesn't pass the test, the commit should be reverted. If the test is passing, then the proper long-term fix would be `delete_sub_interface()` + a targeted single-entry cache removal so we avoid the full rebuild.

> @vpandian-nokia, @deepak-singhal0408, as we discussed in the chassis subgroup, could you add some data to determine if the delay in port up/down notifications from sync to orchagent has any impact, especially during device boot-up?

@arlakshm Port up/down notifications do follow a different functional path. Port state change notifications are handled through the SAI notification callback path in `syncd` and then forwarded to `orchagent`, rather than through the bulk SAI operation path.

In the current `syncd` implementation, `syncd -l` is used for bulk SAI operations toward the vendor SAI and the ASIC. So it does not change the functional `syncd`-to-`orchagent` port up/down notification path directly.

From the current `syncd` implementation, port-state notifications and main ASIC programming work are synchronized through the same `syncd` mutex, so there is no obvious interleaving advantage in the non-bulk case.

In both cases, `syncd` processes both notification handling and ASIC programming work under the same serialized path. The main difference is what happens while that path is held: with `syncd -l`, `syncd` can issue vendor bulk SAI calls for supported batched operations, whereas without `-l`, it still processes the same batch while holding the same mutex, but falls back to handling the entries one by one until the batch is drained.

So based on the implementation, `syncd -l` is more likely to reduce `syncd`-side processing overhead than worsen port-state notification servicing relative to the non-bulk path.

> **Review:** #1774 (second pass)
> 
> Follow-up to my earlier comments (tombstone free-list, `m_failedPolls` cleanup, `addGroup` helper). These are **new** issues, focused on C++ semantics, conventions, and duplication — none overlaps the three above. I read the full file at `3eb7802`, not just the diff.
> 
> Highest-value items inline: **N1** (the `m_failedPolls` tier logic has a real off-by-one _and_ does 3-4 map lookups per failed poll) and **N5** (the erase-then-emplace `m_objectIdsMap` block is copy-pasted 4×, and is reducible to a single `operator[]` assignment).
> 
> Lower-priority polish not worth individual inline threads:
> 
> * **N7** — the `effective_stats_mode` `HasStatsMode<...> ? ... : m_groupStatsMode` ladder is triplicated (addObject / bulkAddObject / collectData); extract `computeEffectiveStatsMode()`.
> * **N8** — map-membership idiom is mixed across the file: `.count(vid)`, `.count(vid) == 0`, and `.find(vid) != end()` all used for the same presence check. Pick one (`.count` reads cleanly pre-C++20).
> * **N9** — the merge branches around lines 2087-2091 / 2120-2133 are over-indented one level vs the surrounding 8-space style (looks like a tab/space mix); a clang-format pass would normalize.
> 
> Nice work overall — the feature is sound and well-tested; these are about tightening the new code before it lands.

Updated the PR description with the full mechanics of the new `-W` flag and pushed the dual-timeout solution. 

One minor issue I want to call out: We have a duplicated 30s default. It now lives once in `CommandLineOptions.cpp` and again in `syncd_init_common.sh`. This has been the case before this PR as well (although we were using hard coded strings in the bash file instead of explicit variables).

It annoyed me for a moment, and I considered just stripping the default value entirely from the C++ side. We can strictly enforce that `-w` *must* be passed, so there is a single source of truth for default values. 

The catch is I'm not 100% sure if `syncd` gets invoked in other test harnesses, unit tests, or obscure paths without the bash wrapper. If it does, removing the C++ default might cause crashes. Given the issue is related to warning messages and does not cause regression / crashes, I'm not sure if the risk is worth it.

For now I am leaving the duplicate defaults as they are. If `syncd` is never invoked elsewhere and we can ensure passing in the `-w` flag (or if there is a more elegant way to extract the default timeout), please let me know.

Hi @Bojun-Feng — I was looking at this more closely and just realized there seems to be a gap in the current approach for the primary scenario from the original issue.

### The gap

The error in [#24803](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/issues/24803) is:
```
ERR syncd0#syncd: time span WD exceeded 30937 ms for create:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_SWITCH
```

This `create:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_SWITCH` is a slow SAI call that happens **between** INIT_VIEW and APPLY_VIEW — as its own event in `processSingleEvent()` with its own `WatchdogScope` (line 415).

Currently the PR applies `transitionToInitWatchdogTimeout()` only around `applyView()` and `inspectAsic()` inside `processNotifySyncd()`. But `create:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_SWITCH` runs outside that — it arrives as a separate event under the normal 30s timeout.

### Why this matters for cold restarts

On any non-warm restart (SWSS crash, `config reload`, device reboot, `systemctl restart swss`), `swss.sh` flushes ASIC_DB:
```bash
# files/scripts/swss.sh:405
$SONIC_DB_CLI ASIC_DB FLUSHDB
```

This removes the `HIDDEN` keys → syncd's `isVeryFirstRun()` returns true → the `m_veryFirstRun` path is taken:
1. **INIT_VIEW** → sets `m_asicInitViewMode = false` (line 4530) — SAI calls go directly to ASIC
2. **`create:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_SWITCH`** → `processQuadEvent` → `processOidCreate` → `m_vendorSai->create()` — this is the slow call, running under 30s normal timeout → **WD exceeded**
3. **APPLY_VIEW** → on very first run, doesn't even call `applyView()` — just flips `m_veryFirstRun = false`

The PR does fix the warm restart path (where ASIC_DB is preserved, `m_veryFirstRun = false`, and the real SAI work happens inside `applyView()`), but the original issue's scenario is cold restart on chassis.

### Suggested fix

Instead of wrapping individual functions, transition the watchdog at the INIT_VIEW / APPLY_VIEW boundaries. This covers everything between them, including `create:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_SWITCH`:

**On INIT_VIEW** (both `m_veryFirstRun` and non-first-run paths): call `transitionToInitWatchdogTimeout()`
**On APPLY_VIEW** (both paths): call `transitionToNormalWatchdogTimeout()` after processing completes

```
INIT_VIEW  ──→  transitionToInitWatchdogTimeout()
                    │
                    ▼
              create:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_SWITCH  ← now under extended timeout
              create:PORT, SET:SWITCH, etc.
                    │
                    ▼
APPLY_VIEW ──→  [applyView() if non-first-run]
              transitionToNormalWatchdogTimeout()
                    │
                    ▼
              steady-state operations  ← back to normal 30s
```

The `inspectAsic()` wrapping can stay as-is since that's a separate slow path.

The existing `transitionToInit/NormalWatchdogTimeout()` functions are fine — they just need to be called at the right places.
